heroTitle: The people closest to a place are often the ones who can regenerate it.
heroDek: We back credible local hubs to grow localism — political, economic, cultural, and ecological — and resource them to do the work their own way.

paragraphs: That conviction runs into a practical problem: the resources to act almost always sit far from the places that need them most.
So the Fund's whole job is to move resources the other way — toward the hubs already rooted in those places — and then to account for what happens, honestly and in public.
mission.objectivesLede: Across every round, the Fund works toward five things:
objectives: Strengthen communities and economies by helping local hubs weave Ethereum-powered tools into their coordination, governance, and resource flows.
Advance Ethereum's adoption and legitimacy through practical, real-world use beyond speculation.
Catalyse tangible local impact across political, economic, cultural, and ecological dimensions.
Build an open knowledge commons so successful local models can be replicated and scaled.
Connect diverse local hubs into one coherent global-local movement for Ethereum localism.

dimensions.lede: We evaluate, fund, and support local programs through four dimensions of localism — a working framework, not a universal definition, but the shared lens that guides our work.

items.name: Political
items.body: Decentralising power toward regional and municipal levels — community councils, participatory budgeting, local referenda.
items.eg: Cities setting their own climate policy when national governments lag.

items.name: Economic
items.body: Local ownership and circulation of wealth — co-ops, local business, community currencies, shorter value chains.

items.name: Cultural
items.body: Local identity, traditions, stories, and heritage over homogenisation — the ties that make belonging and resilience.
items.eg: Bioregional movements linking culture to ecosystem.

items.name: Ecological
items.body: Sustainability and regeneration — community stewardship of land and commons, watershed and bioregional approaches.

items.name: Ethereum
items.body: Credibly-neutral, decentralised infrastructure for community coordination, governance, and resource management — the rails the other four can run on.
items.eg: On-chain treasuries, attestations, and grant rounds.

model.lede: The model is simple: fund the hub, not the project.
paragraphs: Rather than choose local projects from afar, the Fund resources local hubs to act for themselves — they know their place, their people, and what will actually take root. Each hub shapes the work its own way, moves resources openly, and reports back.

Then we read every report against what was promised, trace the money on-chain where we can, and publish an honest account. The aim isn't a scoreboard — it's a learning commons that gets better every round.

rounds.title: Local Grant Programs
rounds.dek: Twelve local hubs across six continents, each given roughly $8k–$18k and four months to run their own local grant program on Ethereum. The full, honest retrospective — project by project.

rounds.title: Local Meetups LATAM
rounds.dek: A lighter, content-first round with the Ethereum Foundation's Ethereum Everywhere team — a $30k pool seeding ten grassroots Ethereum meetups across Latin America.

round02.dek: A lighter, content-first round seeding grassroots Ethereum meetups across Latin America — run in partnership with the Ethereum Foundation's Ethereum Everywhere team.

idea.lede: Where Round 01 funded twelve hubs to run full grant programs, Round 02 goes smaller and more frequent — backing the local meetups where communities actually gather.
paragraphs: Local Meetups LATAM is a partnership between Localism Fund and the Ethereum Foundation's Ethereum Everywhere team. A $30,000 pool supports ten grassroots Ethereum meetups across Latin America for twelve months following Devconnect 2025 in Buenos Aires.

The format is deliberately modest — $3,000 per organiser for a year of consistent, lightweight, content-first gatherings, rather than a few oversized grants. Thirty-seven communities applied; ten were approved in the first wave.

meetups.lede: The first wave — ten meetups across seven countries. Open any one for its location, support, and reports.

items.name: Ethereum Lab
items.place: Santa Cruz de la Sierra, Bolivia

items: $3,000 per organiser for a full year of meetups — roughly $250–375 per gathering.
An initial $750 is released once the organiser completes their Post-Approval Form.
The remaining $2,250 is released quarterly, contingent on activity updates filed via Karma GAP.

status.body: Round 02 is underway. Ten meetups are approved and onboarding; disbursements begin as each organiser completes their Post-Approval Form, then continue quarterly across the twelve-month period. If you run a local community and want to hear when the next round opens, get in touch.

expertsTeaser.lede: Many reviewers weigh every grant — no single authority.
expertsTeaser.body: A peer-attested collective of 36 validated experts — grant-makers, Ethereum builders, and localism organisers — who vouch for one another through TrustGraph, then assess, advise, and amplify the hubs the Fund backs.

chapters.text: Big problems get solved closest to where they happen.
chapters.emph: The money to solve them, almost never is.

chapters.text: So we don't fund projects from above.
chapters.emph: We back the local hubs themselves — and resource them to do the work their own way.

chapters.text: The money runs on open, credibly-neutral rails — reaching communities directly.
chapters.sub: Ethereum is the infrastructure here, not the point.

chapters.lead: We back localism across four dimensions.

items.name: Political
items.note: Power closer to home — community councils, participatory budgeting, local referenda.

items.name: Economic
items.note: Wealth that circulates locally — co-ops, community currencies, shorter supply chains.

items.name: Cultural
items.note: Identity, story and heritage — the ties that make belonging and resilience.

items.name: Ecological
items.note: Stewardship of land and commons — watershed care and bioregional thinking.

chapters.rail: All four carried on open rails.

chapters.text: Then we do the unglamorous part:
chapters.emph: read every report, trace the money on-chain, and publish an honest account — what worked, and what didn't.
Twelve hubs. Six continents. Four months.
chapters.sub: In this round, each hub ran its own local grant program. Some delivered beautifully. Some didn't. All of it is public.

chapters.emph: Ten grassroots Ethereum gatherings across Latin America.
chapters.sub: Run with the Ethereum Everywhere team to back a year of open monthly gatherings — sustaining the momentum of Devconnect in the rooms where communities actually meet.

partners.lede: Localism Fund is a partnership, not a platform — stewarded, funded, and built with aligned organisations.

items.name: Regen Coordination
items.note: regenerative finance, localism strategy, ecosystem connections

items.name: Ma Earth
items.note: matching pool for ecological & community work

deepFaq.a: A nested funding model: resources flow through aligned networks to reach local communities. Thematic rounds fund local hubs, who run their own funding processes and direct resources to local activities. Each hub makes its own allocation decisions — we provide matching pool, evaluation support, and ecosystem connection, not top-down control.

A multi-scalar, polycentric network rather than a hierarchy. Grantees are local hubs anchored in place; the Fund is autonomous but aligned with Gitcoin's Targeted Development & Adoption (TDA) domain (cycles GG24, Oct 2025; GG25, Mar 2026). Subsidiarity cascades through every layer — decisions sit at the most local level able to make them.

Two tiers: three core operators (Monty Merlin · Regen Coordination; Benjamin Life and Patricia Parkinson · OpenCivics) run the rounds and make final operational calls, supported by an attested network of 9–12 expert evaluators who inform decisions, support grantees, and improve tooling.

Local hubs — community organisations, chapters, or coalitions anchored in a place — ready to run their own local grant program: local focus, Ethereum alignment, public-goods orientation, impact + on-chain reporting, operational capacity, and ethical/compliance alignment. Round 01 also required ≥ $5k local co-funding and a qualifying evaluation from ≥ 4 experts.
